---
title: "The Carnot Cycle Reference Page<br/>*Sadi Carnot and the Power of Fire*"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
{{< include /LinkKey.qmd >}}
# Intro to The Carnot Cycle

The Carnot cycle is an idealized thermodynamic cycle that represents the **maximum possible efficiency** for a heat engine operating between two temperature reservoirs.

Although no real engine can achieve Carnot efficiency, the Carnot cycle provides an important benchmark for evaluating the performance of real systems and understanding the implications of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

## Sadi Carnot and the Power of Fire,<br/>the 1824 Edition Part 1

This video introduces the Carnot cycle and explains how its efficiency depends only on the temperatures of the heat reservoirs.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/EDJQAvBIAok?si=99jU5eGibnH6WU66" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](116-Visuals.qmd)

## Sadi Carnot and the Power of Fire,<br/>the 1824 Edition Part 2

This video applies the Carnot cycle concept to systems described using steam tables.

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Qc9W1KKpouU?si=IBy068VJfqv28Oy8" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

[Visuals](116.5-Visuals.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

[Isothermal Process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isothermal_process)  
: A constant-temperature process, $\Delta T = 0$.

[Isentropic Process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isentropic_process)  
: A constant-entropy process, $\Delta S = 0$. Clausius originally used the term to mean adiabatic and reversible, and most thermodynamics texts use the word in this sense.

[Heat Engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_engine)  
: A (usually cyclic) system that converts thermal energy (heat) into work.

Heat-Engine or Thermal Efficiency, $\eta$  
: The ratio of the work produced by a heat engine to the heat extracted from the high-temperature reservoir powering the engine.

$$
\eta \equiv \frac{|W|}{|Q_\mathrm{hot}|}
$$

[Isothermal Heat Reservoir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_reservoir)  
: A reservoir that can accept or reject heat at essentially constant temperature.

Any real reservoir has a finite capacity to accept or reject heat. For example, a well-mixed container of liquid water and ice at atmospheric pressure remains at $0\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$ while the phase change occurs.

[Carnot Engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carnot_heat_engine)  
: A theoretical heat engine that operates with the maximum possible thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{T_\mathrm{cold}}{T_\mathrm{hot}}
$$

where the reservoir temperatures are expressed as absolute temperatures.

A Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes:

1. **Adiabatic (isentropic) compression**, during which the temperature increases from $T_\mathrm{cold}$ to $T_\mathrm{hot}$
2. **Isothermal expansion at $T_\mathrm{hot}$**, during which heat $Q_\mathrm{hot}$ is absorbed
3. **Adiabatic (isentropic) expansion**, during which the temperature decreases from $T_\mathrm{hot}$ to $T_\mathrm{cold}$ 
4. **Isothermal compression at $T_\mathrm{cold}$**, during which heat $Q_\mathrm{cold}$ is rejected


Carnot Efficiency  
: The maximum theoretical efficiency for a heat engine operating between two reservoirs:

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{T_\mathrm{cold}}{T_\mathrm{hot}}
$$

[Quality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vapor_quality), $x$  
: The **mass fraction of vapor** in a saturated liquid–vapor mixture.

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos

### Two videos back

**Resistance is Futile. Entropy Always Wins**  
*Intro to 2^nd^ Law*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/yqVUz1WA7M4), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/IntroTo2ndLawVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/113-Visuals.qmd)

### Previous video

**The ΔS-sentials of Calculating Entropy Changes**  
*Calculating $\Delta S$ for heat reservoirs, ideal gases, and Steam*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/z2e5Rq5r288), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/CalculatingEntropyChangesVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/115-CalcEntropyVisuals.qmd)

### Next video

**How Much Entropy Can You Balance on the Head of a Pin (or in an Open Steady-State System)?**  
*The 2nd Law for Closed, Open Steady-State, and Open Transient Systems*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/QcZTezMtDRk), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/EntropyBalanceEquationsVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/117-2ndLawSystemsVisuals.qmd)

### Two videos forward

**Entropy Made Me Do It: Turbines, Compressors, and Other Second-Law Shenanigans**  
*Turbines and Expanders, Isentropic and Isothermal Compressors*  
[Video](https://youtu.be/Pb7PS5oeCz0), [Info Page](../../balances/entropy/EntropyMadeMeDoItVideoInfoWebPage.qmd), [Visuals](../../balances/entropy/118-TurbCompEtcVisuals.qmd)
